region_of_origin: China (Shanghai / Jiangnan region); widely consumed in Singapore
food_subgroup: Steamed filled buns (baozi) with pork filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class (FOODON_03302050); no specific pork-filled steamed bun subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers steamed bread and buns as a category; no pork-filled baozi-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for steamed pork buns. SNOMED CT does not have sufficient granularity for this prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of steamed pork buns is 3 pieces, weighing approximately 180 g in total, with each bun weighing approximately 60 g.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed pork bun (baozi) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for steamed buns with meat filling
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact USDA entry for Din Tai Fung-style xian rou bao exists. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB entries for steamed pork-filled buns (closest SR Legacy match: 'Bao, steamed, with pork filling') and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for steamed meat buns. Per-100g macros reflect a typical 60% dough / 40% pork filling ratio consistent with published baozi composition studies. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; restaurant-specific formulations may vary.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of steamed wheat-based buns with meat filling — analogous estimate based on steamed white bread / steamed bun GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for xian rou bao (fresh pork steamed bun). GI estimated at 45 based on: (1) steamed white bread GI of approximately 44 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283); (2) the pork filling contributes protein and fat which further attenuate glycemic response relative to plain steamed bread; (3) steaming as a cooking method produces a lower GI than baking for wheat-based products. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 56.7) / 100 = 25.5, rounded to 25.4. Serving carbohydrate = 31.5 g/100g × 180 g = 56.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~684 mg per 3-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit steamed pork bun consumption to one or two pieces per occasion, as a 3-piece serving contributes approximately 684 mg sodium — nearly 30% of the recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg — and should avoid pairing with other high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or chilli.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~25 per 3-piece serving) from refined wheat flour dough
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a 3-piece serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 25, which is in the high GL range; portion control to 1–2 pieces, pairing with non-starchy vegetables, and choosing whole-grain baozi variants where available can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork filling (~2.1 g per 100 g; ~3.8 g per serving) and moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, steamed pork buns are preferable to fried alternatives due to the steaming preparation method, but the saturated fat from the pork filling (~3.8 g per 3-piece serving) and sodium content (~684 mg per serving) warrant moderation, particularly for those managing LDL cholesterol or blood pressure concurrently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed pork buns (鲜肉包, xiān ròu bāo) are a classic Chinese dim sum and everyday snack consisting of a soft, pillowy steamed wheat-flour dough encasing a seasoned minced pork filling. The filling is typically made from fresh ground pork mixed with soy sauce, sesame oil, ginger, spring onion, and a small amount of sugar, producing a savoury, slightly sweet, and aromatic interior. The bun dough is leavened with yeast, giving it a light, fluffy texture that contrasts with the juicy, tender filling. Steaming — rather than baking or frying — is the defining preparation method, which keeps the bun moist and gives it a characteristic white, smooth exterior. Xian rou bao are closely related to but distinct from xiaolongbao (soup dumplings), which have a thinner skin and contain gelatinised broth. In Singapore, steamed pork buns are widely available at dim sum restaurants, hawker centres, and specialty chains such as Din Tai Fung. A standard restaurant serving is 3 pieces (~180 g total). Per 100 g, the bun provides approximately 223 kcal, 9.8 g protein, 6.2 g fat, and 31.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content (~380 mg/100 g) is clinically relevant for hypertension management. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low GI), though the glycemic load per 3-piece serving is approximately 25 (high GL) due to the substantial carbohydrate mass. The pork filling contributes meaningful protein and B vitamins including B12, niacin, and B6. Steamed pork buns are not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets due to the pork content. They contain gluten (wheat) and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central entry for Din Tai Fung xian rou bao exists. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest available SR Legacy / HPB steamed meat bun data and cross-referenced with published baozi composition literature. GI is an analogous estimate based on steamed wheat bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the food type is well-characterised in the literature and the adaptation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the combined bun (dough + pork filling) as consumed. Individual restaurant formulations, particularly Din Tai Fung's proprietary recipe, may differ from these estimates. 2. Serving size of 180 g (3 × 60 g buns) is based on the field survey listing of 3 pieces at SGD 10.07 and is consistent with typical restaurant portion weights for this format. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source database for this food category. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source database for this food category. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source database for this food category. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for xian rou bao was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 5. The food is not halal-certified as it contains pork. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g per 100 g reflects a small residual amount from the pork fat component; this is naturally occurring trans fat (vaccenic acid), not industrially produced. 7. Multilingual names follow Singapore cuisine rules: English (food_name), Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are all provided.
